request.question: Walter W. Arndt translated many poems by the poet and novelist of what dual nationality?

title: Burton Raffel
Xie Zhen (poet)
Rainer Maria Rilke
Multiple citizenship
Banalata Sen (book)
Milorad Pavić
Sino-Indonesian Dual Nationality Treaty
Walter W. Arndt
Madah Kelana
Mexican immigration to Spain
sentences: Burton Nathan Raffel (April 27, 1928 – September 29, 2015) was a translator, a poet and a teacher.
He translated many poems, including the Anglo-Saxon epic "Beowulf", poems by Horace, and "Gargantua and Pantagruel" by François Rabelais.
In 1964, Raffel recorded an album along with Robert P. Creed, on Folkways Records entitled "Lyrics from the Old English."
In 1996, he published his translation of Miguel de Cervantes' "Don Quixote", which has been acclaimed for making Cervantes more accessible to the modern generation.
In 2006, Yale University Press published his new translation of the Nibelungenlied.
Xie Zhen (谢榛, 1495－1575) was a Chinese poet of the Ming Dynasty.
When he was 15 years old, he learned to write poems after Su Donggao (苏东皋).
At age 16, some "yuefu" poems he composed became very popular in Linqing, Deping and nearby.
Most of his poems depicted the landscape of Yecheng, where he lived for many years.
In 1548, Xia Yan and Zeng Xian were executed in a political struggle against Yan Song, and some of Xie's friends were banished from the court in the aftermath, Xie wrote many poems to console them.

René Karl Wilhelm Johann Josef Maria Rilke (4 December 1875 – 29 December 1926)—better known as Rainer Maria Rilke (] )—was a Bohemian-Austrian poet and novelist, "widely recognized as one of the most lyrically intense German-language poets", writing in both verse and highly lyrical prose.
Several critics have described Rilke's work as inherently "mystical".

His writings include one novel, several collections of poetry, and several volumes of correspondence in which he invokes haunting images that focus on the difficulty of communion with the ineffable in an age of disbelief, solitude, and profound anxiety.
These deeply existential themes tend to position him as a transitional figure between the traditional and the modernist writers.

Multiple citizenship, also called dual citizenship or multiple nationality or dual nationality, is a person's citizenship status, in which a person is concurrently regarded as a citizen of more than one state under the laws of those states.
There is no international convention which determines the nationality or citizen status of a person, which is defined exclusively by national laws, which vary and can be inconsistent with each other.
Multiple citizenship arises because different countries use different, and not necessarily mutually exclusive, criteria for citizenship.
Colloquial speech refers to people "holding" multiple citizenship but technically each nation makes a claim that this person be considered its national.
Banalata Sen (Bengali: বনলতা সেন ) is a volume of poems by Jibanananda Das.

During Das's lifetime, it was published twice: first time in Poush 1349 Bengali calendar(December 1942 AD) with a cover by Sambhu Shaha including 12 poems, second time in Srabon 1359 Bengali calendar (1952 AD) an enlarged version with a cover by Satyajit Ray including 30 poems.
Das named the volume after the poem: Banalata Sen, one of Das’s finest poems, certainly his most popular.
The enlarged edition published by Signet Press was awarded in 1953 at the Nikhil Banga Rabindra Sahitya Sammelan (All Bengal Rabindra Literature Convention).
The recurring themes in the poems of this volume are love, nature, time, temporariness of life and love, etc.
Above all, a historical sense pervades everything.
The names that frequent in many poems are Suchetana, Suranjana, Sudarshana and Syamali and these women are deemed above or beyond women in general.
In these poems, the love Das talks about crosses the boundaries of time and place and sometimes seems impersonal too.
Milorad Pavić (Serbian Cyrillic: , ] ; 15 October 1929 – 30 November 2009) was a Serbian novelist, poet, short story writer, and literary historian.
Born in Belgrade in 1929, he published many poems, short stories and novels during his lifetime, the most famous of which was the "Dictionary of the Khazars" (1984).
Upon its release, it was hailed as "the first novel of the 21st century."
Pavić's works have been translated into more than thirty languages.
He was vastly popular in Europe and in South America, and was deemed "one of the most intriguing writers from the beginning of the 21st century."
He won numerous prizes in Serbia and in the former Yugoslavia, and was mentioned several times as a potential candidate for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
He died in Belgrade in 2009.
The Sino-Indonesian Dual Nationality Treaty was a bilateral agreement between the People's Republic of China and the Republic of Indonesia on the issue of the dual nationality of Chinese Indonesians.
It was signed by Zhou Enlai, Premier and Foreign Minister of China, and Sunario, Foreign Minister of Indonesia, on 22 April 1955 during the Asian–African Conference in Bandung.
Following ratification by both parties, the treaty came into force on 20 January 1960 after an exchange of the instruments of ratification in Beijing.
Walter Werner Arndt (May 4, 1916-February 15, 2011) was a world-renowned scholar and translator of Russian, German and Polish.
At the time of his death, he was the Sherman Fairchild Professor of Humanities, Emeritus, of Russian Language and Literature at Dartmouth College.

With degrees in Business Administration from Warsaw University, in Political Science and Economics from Oxford University (Oriel College), a Masters in Engineering from Robert College (Istanbul), and a PhD.

in Comparative Literature from UNC, Chapel Hill, Arndt was well known for his metric translations, which included versions of Goethe's "Faust", Aleksandr Pushkin's "Eugene Onegin", a number of poems by Rainer Maria Rilke, as well as works by Busch, Morgenstern, and others.
His translation of "Eugene Onegin" won the Bollingen Poetry Translation Prize in 1962.
Madah Kelana (or in English is Kelana hymn) is a collection of poems from a poet from Indonesia Poedjangga Baroe Era, Sanusi Pane.
This collection of poems was published in 1931 and is his second poem book.
These poems tells the story of a wanderer who seek happiness and finally found what he was looking for it in itself.
Sanusi Pane made this Madah Kelana after touring India since 1929 to 1930.
The characteristic of this poems are romantic and we can see many poems on the theme of love.
Mexican immigration to Spain refers to the Mexican population in Spain and their Spanish-born descendants.
The Mexicans living in Spain are composed primarily of students, skilled professionals, spouses of Spaniards, as well as Mexican citizens who also have Spanish nationality.

In December 2008, the National Statistics Institute in Spain had 14,399 registered Mexicans within its territory, of which 7,210 hold other nationalities of the European Union or are family members of EU citizens.
To this number must be added those with dual nationality, who are not in Spanish records as foreigners.
Mexican and Spanish laws allow dual citizenship, and many Mexicans who have asked for it, whether they are residents in Spain as grandchildren or they are children of Spanish migrants to Mexico.

In 2010, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Mexico recorded 21,107 Mexicans living in Spain, who became the third largest Mexican community residing abroad, after the United States and Canada; and the largest Mexican community in Europe.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Walter W. Arndt translated many poems by the poet and novelist of what dual nationality?
hop1_docs: The Panther (poem) | The Panther (subtitled: "In Jardin des Plantes, Paris") is a poem by Rainer Maria Rilke written on 6 November 1902.  It describes a captured panther behind bars, as it was exhibited in the Ménagerie of the Jardin des Plantes in Paris.  It is one of Rilke's most famous poems and has been translated into English many times, including by many distinguished translators of Rilke, like Stephen Mitchell, C. F. MacIntyre, J. B. Leishman and Walter Arndt,Jessie Lamont and poets like Robert Bly.

Walter W. Arndt | Walter Werner Arndt (May 4, 1916-February 15, 2011) was a world-renowned scholar and translator of Russian, German and Polish.  At the time of his death, he was the Sherman Fairchild Professor of Humanities, Emeritus, of Russian Language and Literature at Dartmouth College.  With degrees in Business Administration from Warsaw University, in Political Science and Economics from Oxford University (Oriel College), a Masters in Engineering from Robert College (Istanbul), and a PhD.  in Comparative Literature from UNC, Chapel Hill, Arndt was well known for his metric translations, which included versions of Goethe's "Faust", Aleksandr Pushkin's "Eugene Onegin", a number of poems by Rainer Maria Rilke, as well as works by Busch, Morgenstern, and others.  His translation of "Eugene Onegin" won the Bollingen Poetry Translation Prize in 1962.

T. Vasudeva Reddy | T. Vasudeva Reddy (born.21 Dec.1943), a retired Principal and formerly U.G.C (University Grants Commission) National Fellow and Visiting Professor in English from Tirupati, Andhra Pradesh, is a poet, novelist and critic in English.  His poems have appeared in numerous journals and anthologies in India and abroad.  He has authored twelve collections of poems, two novels and three critical works.  His poems have appeared in French journals in Paris and in a recent anthology in Canada.  Many critical articles and research dissertations have appeared on his poetry.  He is now Hon.President of GIEWEC (Guild of Indian English Writers Editors and Critics).  His poems are translated in many languages.

Roy Croft | Roy Croft is a poet frequently given credit for writing a poem titled "Love" and beginning "I love you not only for what you are, but for what I am when I am with you." .  The poem, which is commonly used in wedding speeches and readings is quoted frequently.  It was included in a 1936 anthology entitled "Best Loved Poems of American People", edited by a Hazel Felleman, and published by Doubleday (ISBN  ) and appears without further attribution in "The Family Book of Best Loved Poems", edited by David L. George and published in 1952 by Doubleday & Company, Inc., then of Garden City, New York.  German translations of the poem circulate with the title "Ich liebe Dich" ("I Love You") but are (wrongly) credited to the Austrian poet Erich Fried.  The translation(s) led to the speculation that the poem was just a translation of Erich Fried's work and Roy Croft was a pseudonym used by a translator who wanted to keep all royalties from publication (rather than sharing them with Fried's estate) or who simply did not want to go through the trouble of obtaining a license from a foreign entity.  Taking into account that the poem was already published in 1936 (where Erich Fried was only 15) it seems very unlikely that Erich Fried could be the author.

Prafull Shiledar | Prafull Shiledar (born 1962) is an Indian Marathi language poet resides in Mumbai in India.  He holds degrees in Sciences, Marathi literature and English Literature.  He writes poetry in Marathi – a language in Central India spoken by about 120 million people.  His published poetry books are Swagat (A Monologue) (1993) and Jaganyachya Pasaryat (In the disarray of living) (2006) and many uncollected poems.  His poems are translated in many Indian languages and in English.  His poems are published in National and International journals and anthologies.  He has read poetry in many National literature festivals.

Joseph Auslander | Joseph Auslander (October 11, 1897 – June 22, 1965) was an American poet, anthologist, translator of poems, and novelist.  Auslander was appointed the first Poet Laureate Consultant in Poetry to the Library of Congress from 1937 and 1941.

Piotr Kochanowski | Piotr Kochanowski (1566-1620) was a Polish nobleman, poet and translator.  He belonged to a family of writers.  He was a son of Mikołaj Kochanowski and a nephew of Jan Kochanowski.  He was born in 1566 in Sycyna.  He is famous for his translations from Italian.  He translated into Polish what were generally esteemed to be the two greatest modern epic poems: Ludovico Ariosto's "Orlando furioso" ("Roland Enraged") and Torquato Tasso's "Gerusalemme liberata" ("Jerusalem Delivered").  His version of Tasso's poem served as the Polish national epic.  Piotr Kochanowski was the second poet in Poland (after Sebastian Grabowiecki) to use ottava rima, which became very popular in Baroque Polish poetry.  He died on 2 August 1620 and was buried at the Franciscan church in Cracow.  He is commonly regarded as one of the most important Polish writers of the Renaissance.
response.summary_1: Evidence: Passage [2] says Arndt's metric translations "included ... a number of poems by Rainer Maria Rilke." Passage [1] notes Rilke's "The Panther" (written on November 6, 1902) "has been translated into English many times, including by ... Walter Arndt." Bridge entity: Rainer Maria Rilke (the poet whose poems Arndt translated). Missing fact / next-hop retrieval: Rainer Maria Rilke's dual nationality (biographical descriptor — e.g., "X-Y" nationality). Use queries for Rainer Maria Rilke biography/nationality (e.g., "Rainer Maria Rilke nationality" or "Rainer Maria Rilke was a poet and novelist of what nationality?") to obtain the dual-nationality phrase.
hop2_docs: Rainer Maria Rilke Foundation | The Rainer Maria Rilke Foundation (in French: "Fondation Rainer Maria Rilke") was established in 1986 in Sierre, Switzerland, on the patronage of the municipality.  Its goal is to promote the knowledge of Rainer Maria Rilke's works, through a museum, exhibitions, lectures, conferences, publications and a festival.  The famous poet spent the five last years of his life in the city, living in the Château de Muzot, a 13th-century fortified manor on the edge of town.

Rilke trail | The Rilke trail (Italian: "Sentiero Rilke" , Slovene: "Rilkejeva pešpot" ) is a tourist trail, providing a scenic view of a Gulf of Trieste, named after the Prague poet Rainer Maria Rilke.
Rilke (disambiguation) | The name Rilke is often associated with Rainer Maria Rilke (1875–1926), Bohemian-Austrian poet ("Duino Elegies", "Sonnets to Orpheus").

Rainer Maria Rilke | René Karl Wilhelm Johann Josef Maria Rilke (4 December 1875 – 29 December 1926)—better known as Rainer Maria Rilke (] )—was a Bohemian-Austrian poet and novelist, "widely recognized as one of the most lyrically intense German-language poets", writing in both verse and highly lyrical prose.  Several critics have described Rilke's work as inherently "mystical".  His writings include one novel, several collections of poetry, and several volumes of correspondence in which he invokes haunting images that focus on the difficulty of communion with the ineffable in an age of disbelief, solitude, and profound anxiety.  These deeply existential themes tend to position him as a transitional figure between the traditional and the modernist writers.

The Love and Death of Cornet Christopher Rilke | The Love and Death of Cornet Christopher Rilke is a prose poem written by Rainer Maria Rilke in 1899, revised in 1906, and published in 1912.  Rilke wrote the poem after finding a document in his uncle Jaroslav's papers concerning Christopher Rilke, a man who Rainer's family erroneously believed to be an ancestor and who "died as a cornet in the baron of Pirovano's company of the Imperial Austrian Heyster Regiment of Horse."  The poem recounts the adventures of Christopher Rilke, who travels with a company of soldiers and then, after a night in a castle with a lover, fights and dies in a war in Turkey and is mourned by an old woman.

Clara Westhoff | Clara Westhoff (21 September 1878 in Bremen – 9 March 1954 in Fischerhude), also known as "Clara Rilke" or "Clara Rilke-Westhoff" was a German sculptor and the wife of poet Rainer Maria Rilke.

Rilke Songs | Rilke Songs is a composition for mezzo-soprano and piano by the American composer Peter Lieberson.  The work is set to poetry by the Bohemian-Austrian writer Rainer Maria Rilke.  It was composed for Lieberson's wife Lorraine Hunt Lieberson, who gave the world premiere in Santa Fe, New Mexico on July 18, 2001.  The piece was a finalist for the 2002 Pulitzer Prize for Music.
hop2_titles: The Love and Death of Cornet Christopher Rilke
response.summary_2: Walter W. Arndt translated many poems by Rainer Maria Rilke. Rainer Maria Rilke was a Bohemian–Austrian poet and novelist (passage [4]).
